speaker: interviewer
text: You should look at me, and I will ask a question. So will you introduce us your family, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he will pat you on the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to introduce my family. Well, the-- in one concern, I'm very lucky. Mila and I, we survived the Holocaust. We are blessed with a son who was born on Mh the 20 in 1950 on my birthday as my birthday present. And then 6 years later, Marie was born. Freddie's behind me. Marie is standing next to it. And Judy, who is my numero uno daughter-in-law, is standing on my left side. On my right side is Samantha, my also numero uno granddaughter. She will be 13 years of age in May.

speaker: interviewee
text: June? I'm sorry. In June. I'm very sorry. In June. And he's an extraordinary human being, he knows what means humanity meant to man and inhumanity meant to man. And now on my left side, in my wonderful young fellow, Matthew, my grandson. I adore this boy. We are the best friends. And he's only 9 years old going on 10. And he's a wonderful human being. He knows about-- the most important thing, he went to library a couple of weeks ago and rented the book. You wouldn't believe it what kind. He wants to know exactly what happened, what is written about Grandma and Grandpa. And he loves, too, what he's reading. And I'm very happy man after the tragedy in hell, I'm now in heaven-- in seven heaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you a question, Fred?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know all of this that was happened to your father and your mother. And you know, you know, you was living with them. You know all the activity your father made for Schindler. And, actually, he was in [? Seattle ?] for And what do you think? What-- what kind of experience you are taking from your father's life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing that I really got from what my father has done is-- never give up. And it's so true what he has done for and not only for himself, but all the survivors to get the story told and on record, that this was basically a 50 year quest of his. And as you know, the first nibble was in 1963. And that was 31 years ago. And you know where we're at now. So basically, never give up. That's what I've learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Marie, what have you learned from your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. A lot. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you? What you want to tell us something that-- what do you think about

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never saw the movie. And I think if I saw it, it would be like a good movie. And if I saw the movie, I would learn something from it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and to love people. And like, when people are in trouble, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you should just help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have something to say to that, Samantha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, well, I saw the movie, and it was kind of loud. But it was cool. And I saw-- well, Papa told me that all that-- all that stuff about it. And then I saw it, and it was cool. And I met Jerry. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was made in 1936 in Krynica in Poland, resort area mountain, beautiful resort place. On this picture, I see my mother the second from the left, Mina Pfefferberg, and my sister, Paulina Pfefferberg as a young girl, just after the high schools, and trying to get to university. And the rest is the cousin and the aunts, they're from my mother's side. They went on the vacation in Krynica in Poland. The date of this photo is around 1936, just before the tragedy was struck the Jewish people from 1939. The last name of those people and the family name, but only I give the name of my mother, Mina Pfefferberg, and my sister, Paulina Pfefferberg. The other people's name already faded away from my memory. This picture was taken-- my sister was at this time, 22 years old. It was taken in Krakow, during their vacation time. And this was in 1936 or '38. The exact date I don't know. This picture I received by coincident. About two months ago today, we have a December 6, from a gentleman from Israel, who saw the picture, that I was involved in the story of Mr. Oscar Schindler. And he sent me this picture. And he said that in this time in 1938, he was in big adoration of my sister. He was in love with her. And he-- he dated her for two years. But the war started, they were separated. He survived and lived in Israel. My sister didn't survive. She perished with her husband in 1944, got caught under false paper on the [INAUDIBLE] area paper. And she was executed in Warsaw. My picture--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, was taken in 1938 during the summer activities. I was trying to get the golden pin in athletics. And I received that, and I proudly wear it in my lapel. And this picture was made by a friend of mine. And I'm very handsome. I see how handsome I was when I was-- this was in 19-- I was 25 and half years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very interesting picture. This is the only picture what I have as a teacher with graduating class in-- in June 1939. There was a co-ed gymnasium in [INAUDIBLE]. And this is  a group of those children who just passed the examination with all the teachers who were teaching in the class. And I am here, the third from left in the last row. And people say that I didn't change in the last 55 years too much. Anyway, this was bright, young people there. Some of them survived. And I see this picture, about two weeks ago from Belgium from one of the students who found that I am alive after watching the in Belgium, and she sent me this photo. And I'm thankful to her for it. Because it's the only photo. From this group, there are four girls. This is the girl that sent it, the first girl in the left, the little girl. This is the girl who survived and sent me the picture. Then the third-- my God, you're asking the names. Brother, I have on the tip of my things. I've just escaped my memory. She lives in Belgium. The next one is another student who is third from right in the first row. She's a doctor over there. And her name was [INAUDIBLE]. Now, she's married. I don't recall her married name. And there are other students who stay on the left side of me-- second from the left, [INAUDIBLE]. And he lives here someplace in United States. I don't have his address. Those are the only people what I knew that survived from this group of children. And this was made in [INAUDIBLE] in the front of a high school when I was to privileged to be a teacher in this particular period of time. This very extraordinary picture was made in Bydgoszcz in 199-- in 1934 when I was in the officer's school. This was the first-- we passed the first examination. And we got the first bar as an officer candidates. And I am in the second row-- the-- what was the sixth and second rows-- I am the sixth from the right between the two-- I am the fifth from the right between the two officers sitting in the front. I remember the officer who is sitting in the front to me on the left side is [INAUDIBLE], extraordinary Polish officer. I don't know if he survived. I tried to locate it. He was numero uno officer. And there is the commandant of the school and a general seating next to, on the left side, of the commandant of the school with his son. The three best students were the son of the general who was sitting in the first row on the left side. And another commandant officer in this first row who was second, and I was the third, in the whole group. The first three, we finished at the officer's school Bydgoszcz in the rank of Sergeant Officer candidate in Bydgoszcz, Poland. The school officer was one of the toughest in Poland. And I had a privilege to become an officer of the Polish Army on a special occasion on November 11, 1938. They select on the case of the 20th anniversary of Polish independence after the First World War, they nominated 1,000 new officers to the Polish reserve. And I was one Jew between the 1,000. We received our insignia and our diploma from the hands of the President of Poland, Mr. President Moscicki. And this was on November 11, 1938. And it was a very memorable day for me, because not too many officers Jewish-- of Jewish religion was elevated to this rank in this particular period of time. This photo is a very memorable photo to me, because I became an officer of the Polish Army on special occasion on November 11, 1938, the 20th anniversary of Polish Independence after the First World War. The Polish Ministry of War selected 1,000 officer candidates for this special occasion to elevate them to the rank of First Lieutenant. And I was only the one Jew over there during the ceremony. The uniform, the boots, the saber, the pistol, and the-- all the parts of the uniform I received special payment of 1,000 zloty, what was a tremendous amount of money. The government paid for everything. And this was the most memorable day, because I was proud that I was the only Jew nominated to lieutenant. I have a privilege on account of this to teach also in high school, the ROTC. And this opened tremendous amount of possibility for me to receive a very good job. Because I have a master degree. I was a title of Professor. And I also have a right to teach ROTC, what became a subject in Polish high school like any other subject. Poland was between Germany and Russia. And both the country would like to put their hand and part of Poland and annex it to their country. And I think so the officer-- low ranking officers were very good trained. The high ranking officer was rotten. [LAUGHS] The place of this photo was done in the front of my high school Krakow when I was teaching at Hebrew Gymnasium in city of Krakow, the best school in Poland, scholastically, I claim. The date of the photo is November 1938, by end of November, 1938. I don't remember exact the date. I was nominated on November the 11th, so this was after November 11, 1958. This picture I made in 1974 when I was in Poland when I was interviewed on Polish television. And I showed them the part of the ghetto, the remnants of the war, what was built around the ghetto in city of Podgorze. There's a suburb of Krakow. And there were still pieces of glass on the top of it, because they had covered the top of the wall with lots cut glasses, so people couldn't go over the wall. Otherwise, they cut themselves. Now, there are only this remembrances. Now, it's even less than this left over there in Poland. Because the city was growing up. And that's all that it is-- what is left. But there are still some parts of the war, but we were filming later on in 1993, '94 when we were preparing for the movie, This photo was made by me when I was on one of the visit to Poland. I don't remember whether it was '74 or '82. Because every couple of years, we were going to Poland. Because we have on the Jewish cemetery and Lodz the monument of family of my wife. And we have to keep this in a decent condition. Now, my-- our room was on the second floor above this room going over there. The first two windows on that right side. Why I picked out this particular house when I was looking to where we would be living in ghetto was a good purpose. Because this side of the building was all sealed. The door and the entrances of the lower window was sealed with the cement. Because this part of the side of this part was already Irish side-- not the ghetto side. The house was in the ghetto. Then when you look on the balcony, on the-- on the two windows on the right side, you will see that there is a little like a passage. And next to the corner on the right side is a pipe. As a good sportsman, I was not afraid to go there and slide down and be on the Irish side on the-- on the Polish population side. But I never used the pipe, because we have other ways to get out from the ghetto. This photo was-- this house, we lived in this particular house from March 1941 until March 1943 during the ghetto time. And after the liquidation, we were sent from the ghetto to Camp Plaszow till 1944, and then to [INAUDIBLE]. And then we were liberated on May 8, 1945. The house still is in Poland. And still, a good friend of my family lives in this house who were in the ghetto and lived there, too. This photo is part of the camp in Krakow-Plaszow. The picture was taken by Mr. Raymond Titsch, a-- a Viennese gentleman who was working for [INAUDIBLE]. He was making this photo of camp. And nobody even know why he doing those things. The SS positively didn't know it. Otherwise, they would liquidate him. This looked like he was making them for future use after liberation. I was lucky to get this pictures from him. And we have a copy of the life in the camp. This is beginning of building the camp. This is a part of the SS barracks or some other officers who were there. This picture was taken in 1943. This picture is made also by Mr. Raymond Titsch and is a part of the life in the camp of Krakow-Plaszow, the concentration camp. The photo was taking place, I don't know exactly what year. But this was probably late in 1943. This is when they are bringing-- supposed to be a soup in the morning. And they're giving the spoon of soup. But this was not a soup. This was water with some beets inside, nothing else, no nourishment whatsoever. But the interesting story about Mr. Raymond Titsch, I noticed working in the garage that he was making this picture in a strange way, always behind a corner of a barrack or through a window, so that nobody could see him that he's making this picture. And I, after the war in 1963, I decide to find Mr. Titsch. And I found him in Vienna. I went to Vienna. I met Mr. Titsch. And I asked him, hey, Mr. Titsch, I want the pictures you were making. He denied that he was making any picture. But I got information from my friends in Vienna that his wife is gravely ill and needs a special medication that he cannot get no place. So I went to him again. And I said, listen, I know you have the picture. I'll give you $500. And I put five bills on the table. And I'll buy for your wife all the medication you cannot buy it here, only in the United States. And I send that to you. Give me those photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he started to say, OK, I have these photos. And I give you tomorrow. Why not today? Because they are not-- not at home. We met a date for Friday afternoon around 6:00.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in November, so it was a very dark night. He took me about two blocks away from his home to a little square where it was a monument for some hero of Austria. He digs out a-- in a corner and take a metal box.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he give me my money back. And he said, I give you the film, but I cannot take the money from you. And I said, why? We made a deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't know if those photos are any good. I made this, you know, in 1943 and '44. And they're buried, not developed in the ground. I said, the deal is deal. You keep the money. And I'm taking the photo. Then he said on one condition-- you will not show this photo to nobody that you get them for me. Because he was afraid of the underground of SS ODESSA. I promised to him. And I got a little scared Friday in the evening what do with a hot potato like this. But next day, I decided to go to Israel. And Saturday night, when the flights-- because during Saturday, the flights were not going to Israel, because they are holy day there. I came to Israel about 11 o'clock at night. I took the taxi to Jerusalem. I called my friend from Yad Vashem. And I told him I have film who never was developed. We have to develop the film. Next day in the morning, we developed this film on Sunday morning. And there were three like roles of film-- 106 photo. And they came all in perfect condition, like miracle happened that the photo and the film that was in the ground for 20 years survived that we, the future generation, can see what happened during the period in this particular camp, Krakow-Plaszow, between 1943 and end of 1945. This photo was made in Budapest in-- in September of 1945. We were staying in Budapest. We were trying with Mila to get to Israel. But we got stuck in Budapest, because Russia closed the border between Hungary and Romania. We couldn't get the [INAUDIBLE]. And we must go to back to Germany to try through-- through Germany to get to Israel. And we were staying in a private home. But we were using the famous Hotel Gellert every day for recreational purposes. They have a beautiful swimming pool. And I was a good swimmer. You see my hair is still very short. Because when I was liberated in May, I didn't have-- I was bald-headed. And now, I have a little nice hair coming back. And that picture looks pretty good. I was pretty strong. The place is Budapest, 1945, in September. I found out during the war that my sister was on false paper and hiding someplace in Warsaw. But I didn't know what happened to her and her husband. And her name was-- her husband's name was Eddie Diamont. And Paulina Pfefferberg became Paulina Diamont. They were on false paper in Warsaw. Got-- after the war, I found out they got caught and executed in Palmiry Forest. It's about 15 or 22 kilometers outside Warsaw. I went to this place, it was in 1971. I think so. I don't remember the date. And this is a forest with forest of crosses and tombstone with David Star with no names. So I took a place when it was cross and David's star tombstone next to each other to prove that a Jew and a Catholic can live together when they died in peace. Maybe this gives a symbol that they should live together in peace-- the Jews and any other religion together, not waiting when they get buried. This photo is a memorable photo. This is a photo made in November of 1963 in the Plaza [INAUDIBLE] in Paris when I brought Schindler from Frankfort, Germany to Paris to give him $50,000 and make him, again, respectable Herr Director. Because the money for him was extremely important. He was a very generous man. And the difference, looking on the two faces, I was exactly 50 and a half years old. And he was almost 54. So it was not a big difference in age, but with the face of this man, the happiness in his eye. And  in this time, in 1963, I found out that Schindler don't have even a quarter for to buy a pack of cigarettes. And I got the money from MGM Studio for selling his life story-- the Oscar Schindler story, or Oscar-- a story about Oscar, to MGM Studio. And they pay me $50,000. And I gave it to him, the money. And I brought the money to him in-- to Paris. And I can tell you the story about this moment, an unbelievable story. But they are more private now than anything else. So we leave this to the private occasion. You know-- This photo is showing Oscar Schindler in New York-- in New Jersey, when two of his-- of his survivor of his children, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], became a very successful builder. And they promised when they were leaving Europe that when they will ever have a chance to put a Schindler's street or Schindler's place, they will do it. Now, they've built in 17 town. In every town, are Schindler Place, or Schindler Street, or Schindler Drive, or Schindler Place. In every of the town is his name. And they adore him. This big picture was made in 1971 when he was invited in New York. He was already a few times invited to Beverly Hills by me and a few times invited by other Schindler Jews in New York, or Florida, where they live. This is also a very remarkable photo. I notified the Holy Father about Schindler's story. He knew about this, not only from this particular period of time when I told him about this, but also before. Because he's born in Krakow, in city of Wadowice about 20 kilometers south of Krakow. And he knew about Schindler during the time when he was in the University and after the war when he was Archbishop of Krakow. He became a Cardinal and Pope. And I got invitation to come to Rome. I was on the way from Poland to Rome. And we stopped. And we were invited for Holy Father, who is on the left side. And behind, in the back, is his best friend high school students, high school colleague from public high school since they were born, Jerzy Kluger, who is a distant relative to us. There is my wife and myself, and also his secretary and photographer was there. It was a privately audience. We spent about 15, 20 minutes. I didn't have a present, because I wasn't sure what to do to Holy Father. He doesn't need anything. But I was coming from Poland. And I carry a wonderful book printed in Poland about-- translated, the-- the-- what is-- title of the book was, in a beautiful edition. And when I give it to Holy Father with my dedication, when you're watching his face, how wonderful smile he has. And he thanked me for the book and say, you know Mr. Page, everybody promise me the book, but nobody will give it to me. Thank you very kindly. This was October 1985, a remarkable moment of meeting Holy Father, wonderful human being, tremendous valuable, knowledgeable man. One the best Pope ever in the 2,000 year of Christianity who is sitting over there on the throne. And he works-- not only over there in Vatican, but he goes to every country, kiss the ground, and tell the people that he is the important person who will lead them to freedom.
